---
title: "When Children Bear the Burden"
subtitle: "A UNICEF Data Analysis of Adolescent Mortality and Malaria Diagnostics Worldwide"
author: "Akul Mehta" <br> "DCU Student ID: A00097146"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "📋 Contents"
    toc-location: left
    smooth-scroll: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-align: center
    fig-dpi: 150
    page-layout: full
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: true
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Palette ──────────────────────────────────────────── */
:root {
  --green-dark:   #1b5e20;
  --green-mid:    #2e7d32;
  --green-bright: #43a047;
  --green-light:  #e8f5e9;
  --green-border: #a5d6a7;
  --text-main:    #212121;
  --text-muted:   #555555;
}

/* ── Base ─────────────────────────────────────────────── */
body {
  background: #ffffff !important;
  color: var(--text-main);
  font-family: "Inter", "Segoe UI", sans-serif;
  font-size: 1.02rem;
  line-height: 1.78;
}

/* ── Headings ─────────────────────────────────────────── */
h1, h2, h3 { color: var(--green-dark); font-weight: 700; margin-top: 2rem; }
h1 { font-size: 2.1rem; border-bottom: 3px solid var(--green-bright); padding-bottom: .4rem; }
h2 { font-size: 1.5rem;  border-left: 5px solid var(--green-bright); padding-left: .75rem; }
h3 { font-size: 1.2rem;  color: var(--green-mid); }

/* ── Sidebar TOC ──────────────────────────────────────── */
#quarto-sidebar {
  background: var(--green-light) !important;
  border-right: 2px solid var(--green-border);
}
.sidebar-item-text { font-size: .92rem; }
.sidebar-item-container .active > .sidebar-item-text {
  color: var(--green-dark); font-weight: 700;
}

/* ── Callout / stat boxes ─────────────────────────────── */
.stat-box {
  background: var(--green-light);
  border-left: 5px solid var(--green-bright);
  border-radius: 6px;
  padding: .75rem 1.2rem;
  margin: .8rem 0;
  font-size: .97rem;
}
.stat-box strong { color: var(--green-dark); }

/* ──  note ───────────────────────────────────────── */
.chart-note {
  background: #f9f9f9;
  border-left: 4px solid var(--green-border);
  border-radius: 4px;
  padding: .65rem 1.1rem;
  color: var(--text-muted);
  font-size: .93rem;
  margin-top: .5rem;
  margin-bottom: 2rem;
}

/* ── Code ─────────────────────────────────────────────── */
.sourceCode { border-left: 4px solid var(--green-border) !important; }

/* ── Links ────────────────────────────────────────────── */
a { color: var(--green-mid); }
a:hover { color: var(--green-dark); text-decoration: underline; }

/* ── HR ───────────────────────────────────────────────── */
hr { border-color: var(--green-border); margin: 2rem 0; }

/* ── Footer ───────────────────────────────────────────── */
.site-footer {
  background: var(--green-light);
  border-top: 2px solid var(--green-border);
  padding: 1.8rem 2rem;
  margin-top: 3rem;
  text-align: center;
  font-size: .88rem;
  color: var(--text-muted);
}
.site-footer a { margin: 0 .8rem; display: inline-block; vertical-align: middle; }
.site-footer img { height: 38px; opacity: .85; transition: opacity .2s; vertical-align: middle; }
.site-footer img:hover { opacity: 1; }
.footer-logos { margin: .9rem 0 .4rem; }
.footer-brand {
  font-size: 1.2rem;
  font-weight: 800;
  color: #005b99;
  vertical-align: middle;
  letter-spacing: .5px;
}
.footer-brand.unicef  { color: #00aeef; }
.footer-brand.worldbank { color: #009edb; }
</style>
```

---

## 🌿 Introduction

Every day, roughly **2,500 children and adolescents aged 10 to 19** die somewhere in the world.
Across the 33 years spanning **1990 to 2023**, UNICEF recorded a global total of over **34 million
adolescent deaths** — a figure that dwarfs most publicised humanitarian crises yet receives
comparatively little sustained attention. These are not deaths in the abstract; they are young
people who never completed their education, never raised families, never reached their potential.

This report analyses two interconnected dimensions of child health using UNICEF's global datasets:

- **The geography and trajectory of adolescent mortality** — which countries carry the heaviest
  burden, how the toll has shifted over three decades, and how it divides along lines of sex.
- **The state of malaria diagnostics for young children** — specifically, what fraction of febrile
  children under five receive a confirmatory blood test in malaria-endemic countries. Across
  the 56 countries with available data, the average testing rate stands at just **24.4%** —
  meaning roughly three in four symptomatic children are never confirmed or ruled out for malaria.

Together these indicators reveal a fundamental truth: the risks children face are not randomly
distributed. They are structured by **geography, poverty, and the depth of healthcare investment**.
Understanding these patterns — accurately and in detail — is the first step towards dismantling them.

---

## 📊 Data & Methodology

```{python}
#| label: setup

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes,
    geom_map, geom_col, geom_point, geom_line, geom_smooth,
    geom_text, geom_hline,
    scale_fill_gradientn, scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_x_log10, scale_y_continuous,
    coord_flip,
    theme, theme_minimal, theme_void, theme_classic,
    element_text, element_blank, element_line, element_rect,
    labs
)
```

```{python}
#| label: load-data

# ── Load datasets ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")   # Deaths aged 10–19
ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")   # Malaria testing rate, under-5s
meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")      # Country economic & health metadata

print(f"{'Dataset':<40} {'Rows':>8}  {'Countries':>12}  {'Years'}")
print("─" * 72)
print(
    f"{'Indicator 1 — Adolescent Deaths (10–19)':<40}"
    f" {ind1.shape[0]:>8,}  {ind1['country'].n_unique():>12}  "
    f"{ind1['time_period'].min()}–{ind1['time_period'].max()}"
)
print(
    f"{'Indicator 2 — Malaria Testing Rate':<40}"
    f" {ind2.shape[0]:>8,}  {ind2['country'].n_unique():>12}  "
    f"{ind2['time_period'].min()}–{ind2['time_period'].max()}"
)
print(
    f"{'Metadata — Economic & Health Indicators':<40}"
    f" {meta.shape[0]:>8,}  {meta['country'].n_unique():>12}  "
    f"{meta['year'].min()}–{meta['year'].max()}"
)
```

```{python}
#| label: data-transformations

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# INDICATOR 1 — Adolescent Deaths (ages 10–19)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════

# 1a. Total sex only, cleaned
ind1_total = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .rename({"time_period": "year", "obs_value": "deaths"})
    .with_columns(pl.col("deaths").cast(pl.Float64))
    .drop_nulls()
)

# 1b. Top-15 countries in most recent year — bar chart
latest_year = int(ind1_total["year"].max())
ind1_top15 = (
    ind1_total
    .filter(pl.col("year") == latest_year)
    .sort("deaths", descending=True)
    .head(15)
    .to_pandas()
)

# 1c. By sex, globally aggregated by year — time-series
ind1_sex = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Female", "Male"]))
    .with_columns(pl.col("obs_value").cast(pl.Float64))
    .drop_nulls(subset=["obs_value"])
    .group_by(["time_period", "sex"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").sum().alias("total_deaths"))
    .rename({"time_period": "year"})
    .sort(["year", "sex"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# INDICATOR 2 — Malaria Diagnostic Testing Rate
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════

# 2a. Average testing rate per country (all years, Total sex)
ind2_avg = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .with_columns(pl.col("obs_value").cast(pl.Float64))
    .drop_nulls(subset=["obs_value"])
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_pct"))
    .sort("avg_pct", descending=True)
    .to_pandas()
)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
# METADATA — GDP per capita & Life Expectancy
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════

# Most recent non-null GDP per country
meta_gdp = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col("GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)").is_not_null())
    .sort("year", descending=True)
    .unique(subset=["alpha_3_code"], keep="first")
    .select([
        "alpha_3_code", "country",
        pl.col("GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)").alias("gdp"),
        pl.col("Population, total").alias("population")
    ])
)

# Most recent non-null life expectancy per country
meta_life = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col("Life expectancy at birth, total (years)").is_not_null())
    .sort("year", descending=True)
    .unique(subset=["alpha_3_code"], keep="first")
    .select([
        "alpha_3_code",
        pl.col("Life expectancy at birth, total (years)").alias("life_exp")
    ])
)

# Join for scatter plot
scatter_df = (
    meta_gdp
    .join(meta_life, on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
    .drop_nulls()
    .to_pandas()
)

# Summary
print(f"Transformations complete:")
print(f"  Bar chart   — top-15 countries, {latest_year}:     {len(ind1_top15)} rows")
print(f"  Time-series — global deaths by sex:           {len(ind1_sex)} rows")
print(f"  World map   — avg malaria testing rate:       {len(ind2_avg)} countries")
print(f"  Scatter     — GDP vs life expectancy:         {len(scatter_df)} countries")
```

---

## 🗺️ Where Malaria Testing Fails Children

Malaria kills over **600,000 people annually**, the overwhelming majority of them children under
five in sub-Saharan Africa. It is both **preventable and curable** — but only if it is first
*diagnosed*. Empirical treatment without a confirmatory test wastes scarce antibiotics, masks
the true burden of disease, and leaves other causes of fever undetected.

The world map below shows the **average malaria diagnostic testing rate** per country across all
years of available data (2009–2024): the percentage of febrile children under age five who received
a finger- or heel-stick blood test. A higher value indicates stronger diagnostic infrastructure
and a higher likelihood that sick children receive correct, evidence-based care.

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Average malaria diagnostic testing rate (%) in febrile children under 5. Averaged across all available years (2009–2024) per country. Grey = no data available in the UNICEF dataset."
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 5.8

    # Load world geometry (from local GeoJSON mirror)
    world_geo = gpd.read_file("world_map.geojson")
world_merged = world_geo.merge(
    ind2_avg[["alpha_3_code", "avg_pct"]],
    left_on="ADM0_A3", right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

(
    ggplot(world_merged)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="avg_pct"), color="white", size=0.12)
    + scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors=["#f1f8e9", "#81c784", "#388e3c", "#1b5e20"],
        na_value="#d0d0d0",
        name="Testing\nRate (%)",
        limits=[0, 70]
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1b5e20", ha="center"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9.5, color="#555555", ha="center"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=9),
        legend_text=element_text(size=8),
        figure_size=(11, 5.8),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white"),
    )
    + labs(
        title="Malaria Diagnostic Testing Rate in Febrile Children Under 5",
        subtitle="Average across all reported years (2009–2024)  ·  Grey = no data"
    )
)
```

::: {.chart-note}
**📌 Key insight:** The map immediately reveals two things: the data is concentrated in
**malaria-endemic Africa and parts of South-East Asia**, and even within those regions,
testing rates vary enormously. Sudan leads at **57.4%**, Equatorial Guinea at **45.9%**,
and Zambia at **43.1%**. At the other extreme, Myanmar records just **3%** and Nepal **7%**.
The global average across all 56 reporting countries is **24.4%** — meaning roughly three in
four febrile young children are never tested. Countries shown in grey have no data at all in
the UNICEF dataset, representing a separate but equally serious surveillance gap.
:::

---

## 📊 Countries Carrying the Heaviest Burden

In 2023, the most recent year of UNICEF data, approximately **918,000 adolescents aged 10–19
died worldwide** — a 31.5% decline from the 1,340,000 recorded in 1990. Progress is real,
but the remaining burden is concentrated in a small number of countries.

The bar chart below ranks the **15 countries with the highest adolescent death counts in 2023**.
These 15 nations together account for roughly **60% of all global adolescent deaths** despite
representing a fraction of the world's countries.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-chart
#| fig-cap: "Top 15 countries by total adolescent deaths (ages 10–19), 2023. Counts are across all causes. Labels show exact death counts."
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 6.5

(
    ggplot(ind1_top15,
           aes(x="reorder(country, deaths)", y="deaths", fill="deaths"))
    + geom_col(width=0.74, show_legend=False)
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="deaths"),
        format_string="{:,.0f}",
        ha="left", size=8, color="#333333", nudge_y=600
    )
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors=["#c8e6c9", "#388e3c", "#1b5e20"]
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        labels=lambda lst: [f"{int(x / 1000)}k" for x in lst],
        expand=(0, 0, 0.16, 0)
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=10, color="#222222"),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=9,  color="#555555"),
        axis_title_x=element_text(size=10, color="#333333"),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1b5e20"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9.5, color="#555555"),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor_x=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_line(color="#eeeeee"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white"),
        figure_size=(9.5, 6.5)
    )
    + labs(
        title=f"Top 15 Countries by Adolescent Deaths — {latest_year}",
        subtitle="Ages 10–19  ·  All causes  ·  Source: UNICEF Indicator 1",
        x="",
        y="Total Adolescent Deaths"
    )
)
```

::: {.chart-note}
**📌 Key insight:** **India (133,850)** and **Nigeria (74,173)** together account for nearly
**23% of all global adolescent deaths** in 2023. Population size is a contributing factor, but
it is not the whole story. The **Democratic Republic of Congo (57,349)**, **Pakistan (40,505)**,
and **Ethiopia (29,039)** all rank far higher than their population share would predict,
reflecting the compounding effects of poverty, conflict, and limited healthcare access.
Notably, the **United States (16,388)** appears in the top 15 — driven primarily by traffic
accidents, gun violence, and drug overdoses — a reminder that high national income does not
eliminate adolescent mortality risk.
:::

---

## 💹 Wealth, Health, and Diminishing Returns

A foundational question in global health is how much of the variation in child survival outcomes
can be explained simply by a country's **economic output**. The scatterplot below plots
**GDP per capita** (constant 2015 USD, log-scaled) against **life expectancy at birth** for
208 countries, using the most recent available data from the UNICEF metadata file.

The Pearson correlation is **r = 0.61** — moderate to strong — confirming that economic
development is one of the most powerful drivers of health outcomes. But the **log scale on the
x-axis** tells an equally important story: returns are largest at the bottom of the income
spectrum and diminish sharply as countries grow wealthier.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD, log scale) vs. life expectancy at birth. Most recent available data per country (n = 208). Green line = OLS linear regression with 95% confidence band (r = 0.61)."
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 6.2

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="gdp", y="life_exp"))
    + geom_point(color="#2e7d32", alpha=0.55, size=2.8)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#1b5e20", fill="#a5d6a7", alpha=0.28, size=1.1)
    + scale_x_log10(
        labels=lambda lst: [f"${int(x):,}" for x in lst]
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        limits=[45, 90],
        labels=lambda lst: [f"{int(x)} yrs" for x in lst]
    )
    + theme_classic()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1b5e20"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9.5, color="#555555"),
        axis_text=element_text(size=9, color="#444444"),
        axis_title=element_text(size=10, color="#333333"),
        panel_grid_major=element_line(color="#eeeeee"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white"),
        figure_size=(9.5, 6.2)
    )
    + labs(
        title="GDP per Capita vs. Life Expectancy at Birth — 208 Countries",
        subtitle="Most recent available data · X-axis log scale · Pearson r = 0.61",
        x="GDP per Capita (constant 2015 USD, log scale)",
        y="Life Expectancy at Birth"
    )
)
```

::: {.chart-note}
**📌 Key insight:** The log-linear shape of the relationship is the critical finding here.
A country moving from **\$500 to \$5,000** per capita gains far more life expectancy than one
moving from **\$30,000 to \$60,000**. Countries in the **lower-left cluster** — concentrated in
sub-Saharan Africa — combine GDPs below \$2,000 with life expectancies between 55 and 65 years.
High-income nations in the **upper-right** plateau above 80 years. The scatter of points around
the regression line also confirms that **governance, policy, and public health investment** allow
some countries to dramatically outperform their income level — and others to underperform.
:::

---

## 📈 Three Decades of Adolescent Deaths, by Sex

The time-series below traces global adolescent deaths separately for **male and female adolescents**
from 1990 to 2023. It is the most direct window into whether, and how quickly, the world is
making progress on one of its most preventable human costs.

Two findings stand out clearly in the data:

**The long decline.** Both male and female deaths fell substantially between 1990 and 2015,
driven by expanded vaccination, the scale-up of antiretroviral therapy, improved obstetric
care, and targeted malaria interventions aligned with the Millennium Development Goals.

**The stall.** Since approximately 2015, progress has decelerated. Global deaths edged upward
in 2021 and 2022 before a slight fall in 2023 — likely reflecting COVID-19's disruption of
routine health services and renewed or intensified conflicts in high-burden regions.

```{python}
#| label: fig-time-series
#| fig-cap: "Global adolescent deaths (ages 10–19) by sex, 1990–2023. Each point is the globally aggregated annual sum. The dashed line marks the 2023 midpoint between male and female totals."
#| fig-width: 10.5
#| fig-height: 6.2

midpoint_2023 = ind1_sex[ind1_sex["year"] == 2023]["total_deaths"].mean()

(
    ggplot(ind1_sex, aes(x="year", y="total_deaths", color="sex", group="sex"))
    + geom_line(size=1.25, alpha=0.9)
    + geom_point(size=2.2, alpha=0.8)
    + geom_hline(
        yintercept=midpoint_2023,
        linetype="dashed", color="#aaaaaa", size=0.6
    )
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={"Female": "#e57373", "Male": "#1b5e20"},
        name="Sex"
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        labels=lambda lst: [f"{x / 1_000_000:.2f}M" for x in lst]
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(
        breaks=list(range(1990, 2025, 5)),
        labels=lambda lst: [str(int(x)) for x in lst]
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1b5e20"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9.5, color="#555555"),
        axis_text=element_text(size=9, color="#444444"),
        axis_title=element_text(size=10, color="#333333"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=10),
        legend_text=element_text(size=9),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major=element_line(color="#eeeeee"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white"),
        figure_size=(10.5, 6.2)
    )
    + labs(
        title="Global Adolescent Deaths by Sex — 1990 to 2023",
        subtitle="Ages 10–19  ·  All causes  ·  Source: UNICEF Indicator 1",
        x="Year",
        y="Total Deaths"
    )
)
```

::: {.chart-note}
**📌 Key insight:** In 2023, male adolescent deaths totalled approximately **558,000** versus
**360,000** for females — a **1.55-to-1 ratio** that has remained remarkably stable across the
entire 33-year period. This gap is not biological destiny; it reflects higher male exposure to
road traffic injuries (the leading cause of adolescent death globally), interpersonal violence,
occupational hazards, and in some regions, conflict-related mortality. The dashed horizontal
line marks the midpoint between male and female 2023 totals, visualising the gap's persistence.
The post-2015 stall in progress is visible in both series simultaneously, suggesting systemic
rather than sex-specific causes — most likely the disruption of healthcare infrastructure by
conflict and the COVID-19 pandemic.
:::

---

## 🔍 Key Findings

::: {.stat-box}
**📉 Progress is real, but incomplete.** Global adolescent deaths fell by **31.5%** between
1990 and 2023 — from 1.34 million to 918,000. Yet 918,000 preventable deaths per year still
amounts to roughly **2,500 young people every single day**, the overwhelming majority in
low- and lower-middle-income countries.
:::

::: {.stat-box}
**🦟 Malaria diagnostics remain critically underfunded.** Only **56 countries** report malaria
testing data to UNICEF. Across those countries, the average rate of diagnostic testing in
febrile children under five is just **24.4%**. Sudan leads at 57.4%; Myanmar records just 3%.
The majority of children showing malaria symptoms in endemic regions are never confirmed.
:::

::: {.stat-box}
**♂️ Male adolescents die 55% more often than females.** The male-to-female mortality ratio
among 10–19 year-olds was **1.55 in 2023** — consistent across all three decades of data.
This gap is largely driven by preventable causes: road accidents, violence, and occupational
risks, all of which are amenable to policy and behavioural intervention.
:::

::: {.stat-box}
**💰 Economic development saves lives, with diminishing returns.** The GDP–life expectancy
correlation across 208 countries is **r = 0.61**. The log-linear relationship means the
health returns to economic growth are highest at the bottom of the income spectrum, making
the case for prioritising development aid in the lowest-income, highest-burden contexts.
:::

---

## 📂 Data Sources

The analysis in this report is based exclusively on publicly available data from UNICEF and
the World Bank, accessed via the UNICEF Data Warehouse in 2025:

| Dataset | Description | Coverage | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **unicef_indicator_1.csv** | Deaths aged 10 to 19, by sex and country, all causes | 199 countries, 1990–2023 | [UNICEF Data Warehouse](https://data.unicef.org/resources/data_explorer/unicef_f/?ag=UNICEF&df=GLOBAL_DATAFLOW&ver=1.0&dq=.CME_TMM0T19..&startPeriod=1990&endPeriod=2023) |
| **unicef_indicator_2.csv** | Malaria diagnostics — % febrile children under 5 tested | 56 countries, 2009–2024 | [UNICEF Data Warehouse](https://data.unicef.org/topic/child-health/malaria/) |
| **unicef_metadata.csv** | GDP per capita, life expectancy, population and macro indicators | 211 countries, 1960–2024 | [World Bank Open Data via UNICEF](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator) |

All files were provided as part of **BAA1030 Module, Assignment 2**, Dublin City University
(2025–2026). No external APIs, web scraping, or additional datasets were used in this analysis.

---

```{=html}
<footer class="site-footer">
  <p><strong>Data provided by</strong></p>
  <div class="footer-logos">
    <a href="https://data.unicef.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener" title="UNICEF Data">
      <span class="footer-brand unicef">UNICEF</span>
    </a>
    &nbsp;&nbsp;·&nbsp;&nbsp;
    <a href="https://data.worldbank.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener" title="World Bank Open Data">
      <span class="footer-brand worldbank">World Bank</span>
    </a>
  </div>
  <p style="margin-top:.9rem; font-size:.85rem;">
    Rendered with <a href="https://quarto.org/" target="_blank">Quarto</a>
    &nbsp;·&nbsp;
    Data wrangling: <a href="https://pola.rs/" target="_blank">Polars</a>
    &nbsp;·&nbsp;
    Visualisations: <a href="https://plotnine.org/" target="_blank">plotnine (ggplot2 for Python)</a>
  </p>
  <p style="font-size:.80rem; color:#888; margin-top:.4rem;">
    BAA1030 · Dublin City University · 2025–2026
  </p>
</footer>
```
